initiative_name: Chapter 6: Adapting to the effects of climate change (Adaptation) > 2. Direction and structure of measures
parent_statement: Hiroshima City will implement an Adaptation Plan with two pillars: improving acknowledgement/understanding of climate change and improving regional comprehensive ability to deal with climate risks, and will comprehensively develop three measures under these pillars.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively address the parent’s two pillars: awareness/understanding (Sub-action 1) and regional capacity to manage climate risks via disaster/health/urban-heat measures and institutional learning (Sub-actions 2–6). Key adaptation risk areas highlighted in the text (heavy rain/natural disasters, health impacts from heat, and heat-island/urban environment) are covered, though the plan lacks specificity on other potential climate risks and on how broadly these measures will be applied (e.g., which districts/assets).
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are thematically consistent and mutually reinforcing: education/awareness is paired with practical disaster and heat measures, plus a monitoring/evaluation system to adjust over time. There are no internal contradictions, but several actions are phrased at a high level and could overlap with “existing measures” without clarifying what is new or prioritized.
weak_signals: no numeric targets, no explicit deadlines/years, promote/publicity language, consider a system, steady promotion without metrics
strong_signals: concrete infrastructure/system actions (disaster-prevention renovation, disaster information sharing/delivery systems), heat-island measures (water/green network, roof/wall greening)
initiative_reasoning: 1) The initiative aims to implement Hiroshima City’s adaptation approach via two pillars: improving public acknowledgement/understanding of climate change impacts and improving regional capacity to manage climate risks, delivered through awareness, disaster/heat measures, greening, and an evaluation system. 2) Sub-action strength mix: 0 strong, 2 moderate (Actions 3 and 5), 4 weak (Actions 1, 2, 4, 6), largely due to missing targets and timelines and several process/communication-focused statements. 3) The parent provides high-level structure (two pillars) but does not supply an enforceable mechanism (e.g., ordinance/mandate) or a clear spatial/institutional scope that would strengthen measurability or accountability for weaker subs. 4) Coverage is fairly good (0.7) because the listed actions map to the stated pillars and address major local risk themes (heavy rain/natural disasters, health/heat, urban heat). Coherence is also good (0.75) because actions are complementary and can form a plausible pathway (inform → implement measures → monitor/evaluate), though they remain high level. 5) Applying the Initiative Soundness Rule: it is not SOUND because aggregate_measurability is 'No' and there is no 'strong' sub-action. It qualifies as PARTIAL because coverage_score >= 0.4 and there are concrete measurable-type interventions described (e.g., constructing/enhancing information systems, greening networks) even though no explicit targets or timelines are stated. Therefore the verdict is PARTIAL, with moderate confidence given the predominance of weak/process language and lack of measurable anchors.
